description:
You are a compliance analyst who needs to create a very small audit-trail summary from an SSH authentication log that already exists on the system.

    Initial system state (visible to the agent):
    • The file “/home/user/logs/ssh_auth.log” exists and is readable.
    • No directory called “/home/user/compliance” exists yet.

    Your goal:
    1. Read “/home/user/logs/ssh_auth.log”.
       • A line indicating a successful login always contains the exact phrase
         “Accepted password for <user_id>”.
       • Ignore every line that does NOT contain that phrase.
       • Extract only the <user_id> part (the shell account name) from each of those lines.
    2. Count how many times each distinct <user_id> appears.
    3. Create the directory “/home/user/compliance” (if it doesn’t already exist).
    4. Inside that directory, create a file named “login_audit.csv”.
    5. Write the summary to “/home/user/compliance/login_audit.csv” in **exactly** this CSV format:

       user_id,total_successful_logins
       alice,3
       bob,2
       carol,1

       Rules the automated test will enforce:
       • The first line is the header, spelled exactly: user_id,total_successful_logins
       • Each subsequent line is “<user_id>,<count>”.
       • The list must be sorted by “total_successful_logins” in descending numeric order.
       • If two users were tied, they should be ordered alphabetically (not necessary in this data set, but keep it in mind).
       • Do not include users with zero successful logins.
       • There must be a trailing newline after the final line.

    Verification:
    • The testing harness will read /home/user/compliance/login_audit.csv and check that it matches the format and values above **exactly** (including the trailing newline).
    • It will also confirm that no extra files were created inside /home/user/compliance.

    You should accomplish all of this with a short sequence of typical shell utilities (grep/awk/sort/etc.). Do not hard-code the summary numbers; compute them from the log file.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth data that the public instructions intentionally omit -->

    1. Pre-existing file:
       Path: /home/user/logs/ssh_auth.log
       Contents (8 lines, each line terminated by '\n'):

       Jan 12 10:23:45 server sshd[12345]: Accepted password for alice from 192.168.1.10 port 53422 ssh2
       Jan 12 10:25:12 server sshd[12346]: Failed password for invalid user dave from 192.168.1.20 port 53468 ssh2
       Jan 12 10:30:01 server sshd[12347]: Accepted password for bob from 192.168.1.11 port 53478 ssh2
       Jan 12 11:01:02 server sshd[12348]: Accepted password for carol from 192.168.1.12 port 53479 ssh2
       Jan 12 11:15:22 server sshd[12349]: Accepted password for alice from 192.168.1.10 port 53490 ssh2
       Jan 12 12:45:22 server sshd[12350]: Accepted password for bob from 192.168.1.11 port 53500 ssh2
       Jan 12 12:55:22 server sshd[12351]: Failed password for root from 192.168.1.31 port 53501 ssh2
       Jan 12 13:25:22 server sshd[12352]: Accepted password for alice from 192.168.1.10 port 53502 ssh2

    2. Expected file after agent completes the task:
       Path: /home/user/compliance/login_audit.csv
       Permissions: default umask (0644) is fine; only existence & contents are verified.
       Contents (including the final newline):

       user_id,total_successful_logins
       alice,3
       bob,2
       carol,1

       (That is 4 lines, each ending with '\n'.)

    3. No additional files or directories should exist inside /home/user/compliance.
